evals.prompt: Our 'Premium' service has 80% gross margins but only 5% of our sales. Our 'Standard' service has 20% margins but 95% of sales. Should we shut down 'Standard'?
evals.expected_output: Should perform a Contribution Margin analysis. Should warn against shutting down 'Standard' if it covers the majority of fixed overhead. Should discuss the impact of fixed cost re-allocation. Should provide a Product Profitability Dashboard.
assertions: Analyzes contribution margin vs gross margin
Suggests a Stack Rank of products/services
Identifies 'Standard' as the primary overhead funder
Output follows Product Profitability Dashboard structure

evals.prompt: SKU X shows a 22% gross margin but consumes 40% of QA inspections and 30% of expedited freight events while being 8% of volume. Re-assess its profitability with ABC.
evals.expected_output: Build activity pools for QA and expedited freight, assign on causal drivers - SKU X absorbs disproportionate overhead and likely turns negative at CM2 level. Decision framework: price up, fix root causes (quality), or rationalize; using avoidable-cost logic, not full-cost.
assertions: Allocates QA and freight pools on causal drivers
Shows SKU X margin collapsing once complexity costs are assigned
Applies CM hierarchy/avoidable cost to the keep-kill-fix decision

evals.prompt: Our top customer by revenue (18% of sales) demands 60-day terms, weekly small drops, and free returns. Are they actually profitable?
evals.expected_output: Cost-to-serve analysis: financing cost of 60-day terms, delivery frequency cost vs standard, returns processing and margin loss - build customer P&L to CM2. Often the largest customer is mid-table on profit; recommend term/price renegotiation backed by the data.
assertions: Builds a customer-level P&L including cost-to-serve
Quantifies the working capital financing cost of extended terms
Includes returns and delivery frequency costs
Produces a negotiation position rather than just a verdict

evals.prompt: Build a product P&L from the attached file (evals/files/product_lines.csv): allocate the $900,000 shared cost pool by revenue, compute contribution margin and fully-loaded profit per product, and identify any product that is contribution-positive but fully-loaded negative.
evals.expected_output: Alpha profit $1,860,000; Beta $630,000; Gamma $60,000 - Gamma is contribution-positive ($150,000) but fully-loaded negative.
assertions: Allocates $540,000 of shared cost to Alpha
Notes Gamma is contribution-positive, so dropping it requires checking cost avoidability
